svg.title: How the cross-K function describes relationships between two point types
svg.desc: A three-step scientific diagram. Two point types are identified; type B neighbors within distance r of each type A point are counted, normalized by B intensity, and edge-corrected; then the observed cross-K curve is compared with the null expectation pi r squared and a simulation envelope under stationary spatial independence. Values beyond the envelope indicate unusually many or few cross-type neighbors.

text.tspan: It asks whether points of type B occur unusually near—or far from—
points of type A across a range of distances.
Each location belongs to type A
For every type A point, count type B

tspan.tspan: Repeat for every A point; normalize
by B intensity and apply edge

text.tspan: KAB(r) is the expected number of B points within r of a typical A point,
Finite-sample KAB and KBA estimates can differ.
Use edge correction, an appropriate null model, and envelopes.
